children.name: Pycnonotus
children.description: The typical bulbuls — a large and widespread genus of medium-sized passerines found across Africa and Asia. They have a generally uniform brown or olive plumage relieved by brighter patches on the underparts, head, or ear coverts. Bulbuls are gregarious, vocal birds with rich, melodic warbling songs, and are among the most successful birds in both natural and urban landscapes across their range. Many species are common garden birds, often seen in pairs or small flocks.

children.name: Pycnonotus jocosus
children.description: A smart, crested bulbul named for the vivid red patch behind the eye and the red vent. It is native to tropical Asia but has been introduced widely — to Australia, Singapore, Mauritius, Hawaii, and Florida — where it often thrives in suburban gardens. Its cheerful, bubbly song and bold behaviour make it a popular species, though it can become a pest in fruit orchards.

children.name: Pycnonotus cafer
children.description: A common and aggressive bulbul of the Indian subcontinent, easily identified by its black head, dark body, white rump, and bright red vent. It has been introduced to Fiji, Tonga, Hawaii, New Zealand, and parts of the Middle East, where it is considered a major invasive species, competing with native birds and damaging fruit crops. Its song is a series of cheerful, ringing notes often delivered from an exposed treetop perch.

children.name: Pycnonotus barbatus
children.description: One of the most familiar birds across sub-Saharan Africa, found from Morocco to the Cape and throughout the Sahel and savanna zones. It is a plain brownish-grey bird with a darker face and a pale belly, distinguished by its fluffy crest and bright yellow vent. Its song, a lively 'teacher-teacher' duet between mates, is one of the characteristic sounds of African gardens and bushland.

children.name: Pycnonotus goiavier
children.description: A common bulbul of Southeast Asia, with a distinctive white head and throat, dark eye-stripe, brown back, and bright yellow vent. It is equally at home in gardens, parks, mangroves, and secondary forest, and is one of the most frequently encountered birds in urban areas from Myanmar to the Philippines. Its cheerful, chattering song is a daily sound in tropical cities.

children.name: Pycnonotus aurigaster
children.description: A medium-sized bulbul with a sooty black crown that contrasts with a white cheek and throat, a brownish-grey body, and a bright yellow vent. It ranges from southern China and Myanmar through Indochina to Java and Bali. It inhabits open woodland, scrub, and gardens at low to moderate elevations, and its musical, warbling song is a familiar sound in Southeast Asian towns.

children.name: Pycnonotus finlaysoni
children.description: A distinctive bulbul of Southeast Asia with a bright yellow belly and throat, olive upperparts, and bold black-and-yellow striped throat pattern. It is common in gardens, open woodland, and forest edges from Myanmar to Indochina and the Malay Peninsula. Its active, acrobatic foraging style and cheerful notes make it a favourite species among birders visiting the region.

children.name: Pycnonotus melanicterus
children.description: A striking bulbul of South and Southeast Asia with a glossy black crest, white throat, yellow body, and olive-green back. It inhabits evergreen and moist deciduous forests, scrub, and gardens from Sri Lanka and India through Indochina to Sumatra and Borneo. Its loud, musical whistles are often the first indication of its presence in dense canopy vegetation.

children.name: Pycnonotus plumosus
children.description: A dull-olive bulbul with a greyish breast and a distinctive olive wash on the wings and tail. It ranges from Myanmar through the Malay Peninsula to Sumatra, Borneo, and the Philippines. It inhabits lowland forests, forest edges, and mangroves, often in pairs or small flocks foraging in the mid-storey. Its song is a series of short, mellow whistles.

children.name: Pycnonotus simplex
children.description: A plain, unstreaked bulbul with brownish-grey upperparts, whitish underparts, and a distinctive creamy-yellow vent. Found in the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Borneo, and Java in lowland forests and secondary growth. It is often overlooked due to its drab appearance but has a distinctive, cheerful warbling song.

children.name: Pycnonotus xanthorrhous
children.description: A medium-sized bulbul with a brown breast band, grey-brown upperparts, and a white belly with a yellow vent. It is found from the central Himalayas through southern China to northern Myanmar and Indochina. Inhabits scrub, open forests, and gardens at moderate elevations, where its melodic, warbling song is frequently heard.

children.name: Pycnonotus sinensis
children.description: A common bulbul of East Asia with a distinctive white nape patch, black crown, olive-green upperparts, and a white vent. Ranges from central and eastern China to Taiwan and northern Vietnam. It is one of the most familiar garden birds in Chinese cities, known for its loud, varied song and bold behaviour.

children.name: Pycnonotus leucotis
children.description: A handsome bulbul with a black head, white cheek patch, pale grey-brown body, and yellow vent. Found from the Middle East through Iran, Afghanistan, Pakistan, and northwestern India. Inhabits dry scrub, palm groves, and gardens. Its song is a rich, melodious warble, and it is a common bird in urban areas throughout its range.

children.name: Pycnonotus leucogenys
children.description: A crested bulbul of the Himalayas, with a brownish-grey body, black forehead, white cheeks, and bright yellow vent and undertail. It is common in wooded areas, gardens, and scrub from Afghanistan through the Himalayas to Myanmar. Its lively, bubbling song is one of the characteristic sounds of Himalayan hill stations.

children.name: Pycnonotus xantholaemus
children.description: A striking yellow-throated bulbul endemic to the rocky scrub forests of the Eastern Ghats and central Indian hills. Olive-green above with a bright yellow throat and vent, grey breast, and a yellow eye-ring. It is classified as Vulnerable due to habitat loss and is a sought-after species for birders visiting southern India.

children.name: Pycnonotus flavescens
children.description: A pale yellow-brown bulbul with a faint yellowish wash on the underparts, a dark moustachial line, and a warm brown back. Found from the eastern Himalayas through Southeast Asia to Sumatra and Borneo. Inhabits montane forests, scrub, and clearings at elevations above 1,000 metres.

children.name: Pycnonotus snouckaerti
children.description: A recently described bulbul endemic to the montane forests of northern Sumatra, in the Aceh province. It has olive-green upperparts, a yellow throat and belly, and a distinctive greyish head. Restricted to a small area of hill forest above 800 metres, it is considered Near Threatened due to ongoing habitat loss.

children.name: Pycnonotus bimaculatus
children.description: A colourful bulbul endemic to the Sunda region, with olive upperparts, bright yellow underparts, and a distinctive orange spot on the ear coverts. Found in Sumatra, Java, Bali, and Borneo. Inhabits montane forests and forest edges at elevations between 800 and 2,500 metres. Its cheerful song is a common sound in Javan hill stations.

children.name: Pycnonotus tympanistrigus
children.description: A medium-sized bulbul endemic to the montane forests of western Sumatra. It has a dark brown head with a conspicuous white spot on the lower throat and a white crescent below the eye, giving it a distinctive facial pattern. Olive-brown above, pale below. Restricted to elevations above 900 metres in the Barisan Mountains.

children.name: Pycnonotus cyaniventris
children.description: A rather plain bulbul with greyish-brown upperparts and a distinctly grey belly and vent, lacking the yellow undertones of most relatives. Found in the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo in lowland and hill forests. It is a quiet, unobtrusive species that forages in the mid-storey, often in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Pycnonotus urostictus
children.description: A distinctive bulbul endemic to the Philippines, where it is found on Luzon, Catanduanes, and nearby islands. It has a prominent yellow wattle around the eye, olive-green upperparts, and a yellow vent. Inhabits lowland and hill forests, forest edges, and secondary growth. Its call is a series of clear, ringing whistles.

children.name: Pycnonotus nieuwenhuisii
children.description: A poorly known bulbul endemic to the montane forests of northern Borneo. It has a distinctive blue wattle around the eye, dark olive upperparts, and a pale grey belly. Known from only a handful of specimens and sightings in the Kelabit Highlands and Mount Kinabalu area. The species is named after the Dutch explorer Anton Nieuwenhuis.

children.name: Acritillas
children.description: The yellow-browed bulbul — a monotypic genus of small, active bulbul confined to the Western Ghats and hills of southern India. It is distinguished from other bulbuls by its bright yellow brow and underparts, and is one of the most colourful members of the family in the Indian subcontinent. It forages actively in the mid-storey of forests, often in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Acritillas indica
children.description: A glossy olive-green bulbul with bright yellow underparts and a prominent yellow brow stripe above the eye. It is endemic to the Western Ghats and associated hill ranges of southern India, where it inhabits wet evergreen and shola forests. Its rapid, metallic song — a series of ringing notes — is a characteristic sound of the Western Ghats highlands, where it is one of the most common endemic birds.

children.name: Iole
children.description: A genus of medium-sized, olive-brown bulbuls found in Southeast Asia. They are duller-plumaged than Pycnonotus, lacking bright head or vent colouration, and are primarily forest-dwelling. Their songs are less musical than those of the typical bulbuls, consisting of harsh, chattering notes. They forage in the mid-storey and canopy of lowland and hill forests.

children.name: Iole propinqua
children.description: A dull olive-brown bulbul with a distinctive pale grey iris that gives it its name. Found from southern China and Myanmar through Indochina to the Malay Peninsula. It inhabits evergreen and mixed deciduous forests, often in the canopy, where its harsh, grating calls reveal its presence.

children.name: Iole crypta
children.description: A rather plain olive-brown bulbul with a buff-coloured vent and a dark eye. Found in the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo in lowland and hill forests. It is secretive and more often heard than seen, with a song consisting of low, chattering whistles.

children.name: Iole charlottae
children.description: A medium-sized bulbul with olive-brown upperparts and a pale buff belly, formerly grouped with the Buff-vented Bulbul but split on vocal and genetic grounds. Found in the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. Inhabits lowland and hill forests, where its chattering call is a frequent sound in the mid-storey.

children.name: Iole cacharensis
children.description: A plain olive-brown bulbul restricted to the Cachar Hills of northeastern India and adjacent Bangladesh. It is a poorly known species that inhabits evergreen forest and dense scrub, where it forages quietly in the understorey. Its song is a low, chattering warble that betrays its presence in dense cover.

children.name: Iole finschii
children.description: A small, olive-brown bulbul with a pale grey throat and breast, found in the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. It inhabits lowland and hill forests, often foraging in the canopy in small groups. Named after the German naturalist Friedrich Hermann Otto Finsch. Its harsh, chattering call is frequently heard in the forest interior.

children.name: Alcurus
children.description: A small genus of bulbuls once included in Pycnonotus, distinctive for their heavily streaked plumage. The Striated Bulbul is the best-known member, recognised by the bold white streaking on its throat and breast. They are birds of montane forests and scrub in South and Southeast Asia, where their distinctive calls are a common sound in hill stations.

children.name: Alcurus striatus
children.description: A distinctive bulbul with bold white streaking on the throat, breast, and flanks, olive-brown upperparts, and a dark crest. It is found from the eastern Himalayas through Myanmar to southern China, Thailand, and Indochina. Inhabits montane forests and scrub at elevations above 1,500 metres, often in noisy flocks foraging in fruiting trees.

children.name: Hemixos
children.description: A small genus of Asian bulbuls with subtly beautiful plumage in shades of olive, grey, and yellow. They inhabit montane and foothill forests, where their musical songs are a characteristic sound. The genus was once merged with Hypsipetes but has been reinstated based on molecular evidence.

children.name: Hemixos flavala
children.description: A medium-sized bulbul with ashy-grey upperparts, a whitish throat, and pale yellow underparts, with a distinctive dark moustachial stripe. Found from the eastern Himalayas through Southeast Asia to Sumatra and Borneo. Inhabits evergreen forests and forest edges at middle elevations, often in mixed-species flocks. Its song is a lively series of whistling notes.

children.name: Hemixos cinereus
children.description: A rather drab, uniformly greyish-brown bulbul with a pale throat and a slight yellowish wash on the belly. Found on the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo in lowland and hill forests. It is an unobtrusive species that forages quietly in the mid-storey, and its subdued colouration makes it one of the most easily overlooked bulbuls in its range.

children.name: Hypsipetes
children.description: A widespread genus of bulbuls found across Africa, tropical Asia, and islands of the Indian Ocean. They are generally larger and darker than Pycnonotus, often with black, grey, or dark brown plumage and bright orange or red bills. They are predominantly arboreal and frugivorous, with loud, raucous calls that carry through the forest canopy.

children.name: Hypsipetes leucocephalus
children.description: A large, entirely black or dark grey bulbul with a striking bright orange-red bill and legs, and a shaggy crest. Ranges from the Himalayas through southern China to Southeast Asia. It inhabits montane and foothill forests, forming noisy flocks that descend on fruiting trees with loud, squeaky calls.

children.name: Hypsipetes ganeesa
children.description: A dark grey bulbul with a square-shaped tail and a bright orange-red bill, very similar to the Black Bulbul but with a squarer tail and differences in vocalisation. Found in the Western Ghats of India and Sri Lanka. Inhabits montane evergreen forests and plantations, often in noisy flocks in fruiting trees.

children.name: Hypsipetes philippinus
children.description: A large, dark bulbul endemic to the Philippines, with a dark grey body, a blackish head, and a bright orange-red bill and legs. Found across many of the Philippine islands in montane and lowland forests. It is a noisy, conspicuous bird that gathers in flocks in fruiting trees, its raucous calls echoing through the forest canopy.

children.name: Hypsipetes rufigularis
children.description: A distinctive bulbul endemic to the Zamboanga Peninsula of Mindanao in the Philippines. It has a rufous throat and vent contrasting with dark grey upperparts and a paler belly, with a bright orange bill. Restricted to lowland and hill forests in a small range, it is classified as Vulnerable due to deforestation.

children.name: Hypsipetes siquijorensis
children.description: A medium-sized bulbul endemic to the Visayan islands of the Philippines, with dark brown upperparts, a pale throat, and heavily streaked whitish underparts. It inhabits lowland forests and forest edges on the islands of Siquijor, Cebu, and Negros. It is classified as Endangered due to extensive deforestation across its limited range.

children.name: Hypsipetes everetti
children.description: A medium-sized bulbul endemic to the Philippines, with olive-brown upperparts, pale underparts, and a distinctive white throat and eye-ring. Found in lowland and hill forests across many islands. Named after the British colonial administrator and naturalist Alfred Hart Everett. It is a noisy, social species often found in small flocks.

children.name: Hypsipetes maclellandii
children.description: A handsome bulbul of montane forests from the Himalayas through Myanmar to southern China and Indochina. It has a dark brown or blackish head, chestnut-brown body, and a bright orange-red bill. It is common in hill forests above 1,000 metres, where its loud, squeaky calls are a characteristic sound of the upper canopy.

children.name: Spizixos
children.description: A small genus of two species of distinctive, finch-like bulbuls found in East and Southeast Asia. They have stout, conical bills and a greenish or olive plumage, and are unique among bulbuls in having a serrated bill edge. They are highly social, forming noisy flocks in forest edges and clearings.

children.name: Spizixos canifrons
children.description: A distinctive, olive-green bulbul with a prominent forward-curving crest, a stout pale bill, and a yellow throat. Found from the eastern Himalayas through Myanmar to southern China and northern Indochina. Inhabits forest edges, scrub, and clearings at middle elevations, often in noisy flocks foraging in fruiting shrubs.

children.name: Spizixos semitorques
children.description: A greenish-olive bulbul with a dark collar across the upper breast, a stout pale bill, and a short crest. Found in southern China, Taiwan, and northern Vietnam. Inhabits scrub, secondary forest, and forest edges at low to middle elevations. Its song is a series of loud, rapid whistling notes, and it is often seen in small, active flocks.

children.name: Tricholestes
children.description: A monotypic genus of bulbul found in Southeast Asia. The Hairy-backed Bulbul is characterised by its shaggy, hair-like feathers on the back and nape, giving it a distinctly unkempt appearance. It is a forest-dwelling species that forages in the understorey and mid-storey, often in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Tricholestes criniger
children.description: A small, shaggy-looking bulbul with a rufous-brown back, pale underparts, and distinctive hair-like feathers on the nape and upper back that give it a ruffled appearance. Found in the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Borneo, and Java in lowland and hill forests. It is a quiet, unobtrusive bird that forages in the understorey, often in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Setornis
children.description: A monotypic genus of bulbul endemic to Southeast Asia, distinguished by its unusually long, hook-tipped bill — the longest and most decurved of any bulbul. It inhabits lowland peat swamp forests and is dependent on this threatened habitat.

children.name: Setornis criniger
children.description: A medium-sized, brownish-olive bulbul with a remarkably long, curved, hook-tipped bill — the most extreme bill shape in the family. Found in the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo, where it is restricted to lowland peat swamp and heath forests. Its specialised bill allows it to probe deep into bark crevices and epiphytes for insects. Classified as Vulnerable due to destruction of its peat swamp habitat.

children.name: Alophoixus
children.description: A genus of medium-sized, crested bulbuls of Southeast Asian forests, with stout bills and olive-brown or greyish plumage. They are primarily frugivorous and often gather in noisy flocks at fruiting trees. Their loud, chattering calls are characteristic of lowland and hill forests across the region.

children.name: Alophoixus bres
children.description: A medium-sized, olive-brown bulbul with a distinctive greyish-white cheek patch, a pale throat, and a slight crest. Found in Java, Bali, and the Lesser Sundas. Inhabits lowland and hill forests, forest edges, and secondary growth. It is a noisy, social bird that gathers in small flocks in fruiting trees.

children.name: Alophoixus flaveolus
children.description: A drab olive-brown bulbul with a yellowish tinge to the underparts and a pale throat. Found from the eastern Himalayas through Myanmar to Thailand and Indochina. Inhabits evergreen and mixed deciduous forests at low to middle elevations. Its song is a series of rich, warbling notes, and it often joins mixed-species foraging flocks.

children.name: Alophoixus phaeocephalus
children.description: A handsome bulbul with olive-green upperparts, a bright yellow belly, a grey head, and a prominent white throat. Found in the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo in lowland and hill forests. It is an active and noisy bird, often seen in mixed-species flocks foraging in the mid-storey and canopy.

children.name: Alophoixus pallidus
children.description: A medium-sized bulbul with a strikingly pale, whitish face and throat contrasting with olive-brown upperparts and a brown breast band. Found from Myanmar through Thailand and Indochina to southern China. Inhabits evergreen and deciduous forests, often in noisy flocks in the forest canopy.

children.name: Alophoixus ruficrissus
children.description: A recently recognised species split from the Grey-cheeked Bulbul, found in Borneo and the Natuna Islands. It has a rufous-chestnut vent and undertail coverts, olive-brown upperparts, and a greyish face. Named after the Penan people of Borneo. Inhabits lowland and hill forests, where it is a common but often overlooked member of mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Brachypodius
children.description: A small genus of Asian bulbuls with short, stout bills, formerly placed in Pycnonotus. They are predominantly black-headed or dark-capped with bright yellow or olive bodies, and are common in lowland forests and secondary growth across South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Brachypodius melanocephalos
children.description: A glossy black-headed bulbul with a yellow body, olive-green back, and a short, stout bill. Widespread from the Indian subcontinent through Southeast Asia to the Philippines and Indonesia. Inhabits lowland forests, mangroves, and gardens, often in noisy flocks. It is one of the most common bulbuls in Southeast Asian lowlands.

children.name: Brachypodius priocephalus
children.description: A small, attractive bulbul with a grey head and throat, olive-green upperparts, and bright yellow underparts. Endemic to the Western Ghats of India, where it inhabits evergreen and moist deciduous forests. It is a shy, unobtrusive species that forages in the understorey and is classified as Near Threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Microtarsus
children.description: A small genus of pied bulbuls of Southeast Asia, with striking black-and-white plumage unlike any other members of the family. They are active and conspicuous birds of lowland forests and forest edges.

children.name: Microtarsus melanoleucos
children.description: A striking, boldly patterned bulbul with entirely black upperparts and head, and pure white underparts — the only pied bulbul in its range. Found in the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. Inhabits lowland forests, forest edges, and plantations. It is an active and conspicuous bird, often seen in pairs or small groups in the mid-storey and canopy.

children.name: Rubigula
children.description: A genus of black-crested, flame-throated bulbuls of South and Southeast Asia, formerly included in Pycnonotus but separated based on molecular evidence. They are characterised by glossy black crests and bright yellow, red, or orange throats.

children.name: Rubigula flaviventris
children.description: A striking bulbul with a glossy black crest and head, bright yellow body, and olive-green back. Found from the Himalayas through Myanmar, Thailand, and Indochina to the Malay Peninsula. Inhabits evergreen forests, secondary growth, and gardens, and is one of the most conspicuous and vocal bulbuls in Southeast Asian forests.
